candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be made predictively discriminating rather than post hoc? Specifically, what observable structural variables and threshold conditions distinguish cases where a disruption will settle into broad-sharing versus concentration, and how do these variables interact across technologies and sectors?

What is the causal hierarchy among the proposed load-bearing variables—diffusion costs, bargaining power, openness of complements, ownership dispersion, and coordination capacity? Are some primary generators of the equilibrium while others are downstream symptoms, and under what conditions can changing one variable durably shift the whole system rather than trigger compensating adaptations?

What mechanisms can overcome coordination failures at the relevant scale without destroying innovation incentives or simply relocating concentration elsewhere? In particular, which institutional interventions actually change individually rational strategies for firms, workers, investors, and states, rather than merely redistributing gains temporarily within the same centralizing equilibrium?
